item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The first image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from the Smithsonian Institution which only allows for non-commercial use of their images. Thus, the image has been removed. The second image is an edited version of this image and is cc-compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked reading this article. SCP-2894 is, in another reality prespective a completely different SCP which solution to the haltering resulted in the shatter of precious information and time itself. Project Metatron has altered our reality, and Sandy Island is what is left of this reality changing event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Other than that, this is pretty well made! *upvotes*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I clicked "edit" to learn how the page was laid out the way it was. I didn't change it.

item.title: Working out article superblackboxes...
item.content:encoded:
Progress can be viewed at http://scpsandbox2.wikidot.com/yourbuddybill for now.
I dunno why I decided to do it with this one in particular. Hope it helps people understand what's going on here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've seen quite a few articles use 05 instead of O5, but yeah it should technically be O5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the use of 05-Blackbox in the Metatron message a typo? Normally we use O5-Blackbox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This phrase made me upvote:

"Supercavitating subsurface electromagnetic accelerator cannon"

I'm naming my firstborn child that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's one of the things I was implying. We don't know how much was erased, and there could be some serious consequences that aren't yet apparent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not the greatest fan of skips that try to explain a real world thing, but this somewhat works. It was interesting enough that I'm upvoting, though as it was said before there could be more development.
Two points in particular:
1) The dimensional hole: how did it appear? Given the flow of water, it must have suddenly appear just before it was noticed. And it's true that the Foundation may not have a way of knowing its origin; but for being the direct cause of an end-of-the-world scenario there was a bit too little concern about it.
2) The consequences of Metatron: it was said that executing it in its current phase could have disastrous consequences, but in the end the only deleterious thing that happened was the loss of the project and some corrupted documentation.
(There is some fridge logic here, though; in particular, taking the warning at face value, there could be a lot of now-uncontained skips around, which is severe enough).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because that portion of the data was corrupted the same way and reused in the database.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an island that doesn't exist which contains a portal to the bottom of an alien sea that is going to flood our planet. Also there's weird time travel stuff going on? This is kind of ambitious world building.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If most of the black-boxing is due to data corruption, then why does " SCP-██3█" always have the same digits missing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is brilliant, and have upvoted accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holland is actually an area that is split up into two provinces, Noord Holland and Zuid Holland (North and South Holland respectively). There are also other provinces which lie under sea level, such as Zeeland, Groningen and parts of Utrecht. Considering the extreme amount of seawater pouring in, in due time the entirety of the Netherlands would be flooded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I made the edit you suggested

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It isn't wrong if they're referring to the province.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, before I forget: the use of Holland is wrong here. Holland is only a province of the Netherlands; a professional organization like the Foundation would use 'The Netherlands' instead of Holland.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find this article difficult to judge; on one hand the concept is quite interesting, but on the other it feels overdone. The actual object itself is actually quite boring, being just a hole that happens to link to the bottom of an extraterrestrial ocean. However, what makes this thing a bit more interesting is the Foundation's escalating reaction to it. The Metatron-device is therefore quite interesting.

When you first look at it, at least. After you understand what the thing is, it is just another temporal distortion machine that broke it's timeline and did it's job too well. It is very comparable to SCP-2069. The two devices have both destroyed their respective timelines, and their debris has appeared in our timelines. The interesting thing with this one is, that the document itself is the debris. The blackboxing was actually very readable for me, and I was able to fill in the words as I was going along.

However, I still find the general plot-device here (unavoidable catastrofe, employ timeline alterator, destroy self) too generic, but this is mitigated somewhat by what the document actually is. I'm going to abstain from voting here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was mentioned before but they are different pictures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The island looks remarkably like the one from SCP-930.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Instead of replying to your own comments, you can edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Instead of replying to your own comments, you can edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm missing something but I don't really see how they are similar

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would almost like this (the writing has some gaps, some less-than-professional spots, regardless) if I hadn't written something pretty similar in plot a couple of years ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: a matter of*, not or…stupid phone ;_;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just to address the complaints about the black boxing…it's the result of this fragment of the project documentation being all that's left of the project itself. I can't think of a less disruptive way to convey this using the site's format, and personally it *really* works.

I think some of the complaints are just a matter or personal taste, from what I've read in the comments over the years. Some of us just don't like things in a certain way, on a certain subject, etc. But some of us do. I myself really like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: amount*, not amoun…hate typing via phone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't feel overwhelmed at all. I got the idea of it completely by the end - Metatron-13 was needed, but because it wasn't perfected, it eliminated itself and a great deal of information pertaining to it, along with the original SCP threat. So the original "Neutralized" status isn't a smokescreen, it's the result of Metatron-13, which no longer exists (or existed).

I think the amoun of blackout is dead-on. Works really well for a project that's corrupted because of a reality restructuring, rather than a simple 'corruption.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I too like this in concept. One problem lies in how to show a corrupted document; the blackboxes are nice, but their very nature (solid black space) draws the eye to them, and interrupts the flow of reading. You could use a combination of dashes, ellipses and gibberish, but that would not lessen the frustration the reader gets from the lack of information. The other way would be to quote the person(s) who discovered these entries over the source document. Not sure either would significantly improve the article as a whole.

I really do like the fact that we have an unsolvable dilemma resolved by an unproved/provable device. The foundations viscous circle of "damned if we do." makes for much entertaining pondering. +1 for great idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one! Just one minor thing here -

deleted fromm the Foundation database,

I think there's an extra m there, unless you meant for it to be like this. Other than that, I agree that the blackboxing is needed, and I think it leaves just enough out. +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's just something here I'm not feeling. The last collapsible is too unreadable for me and by the time I get there, I'm forcing myself to read through it. Nothing is there in the beginning to carry me all the way through. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took an image I found on the Internet and applied a few image filters in GIMP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually really liked this one, and I'm a bit of a dissenting voice in that I really liked the black-boxing technique here to get across that the document/information was corrupted. Totally see where you were going with the black-boxing narratively. It's a +1 for me, I can't really think of anything that I would change - although a tale to go along with it might be quite nice?

I suppose I especially like this because I've just read Flood by Stephen Baxter & this can be my new headcanon for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The black boxes in this case are not meant to obscure information, but to represent the damaged parts of the document. We're suppposed to recieve the context, instead of speculation. Although black boxes are not used in the typical format as redactions, the use as a literary device still seems acceptable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to go with a downvote.

The elements are so familiar I'm getting a very strong feeling of deja vu. I think for such a… kind of "Old Standard" SCP story, it doesn't really particularly stand out, and it would really need to.

My biggest problem is with the black-boxing in the Level 5 document in general. In the Thaumiel SCP and especially in the notes between the Site Director and the O5s, it definitely feels like the entire context is given despite the black-boxing and that there is nothing mysterious or interesting that could possibly be hiding in the corruptions/redactions. If they don't invite speculation, redactions are completely superfluous.

The other thing is that it seems that you have two narrative hooks. One is a world-threatening catastrophe, and the other is an object that changes history but could potentially have dire space-time consequences. In the current state, the world-threatening catastrophe is completely averted, and you've given no evidence or insinuation of serious consequences of Metatron except for Metatron and the island not existing. Things would have to get way weirder than that for this to stand out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotta agree here. I really liked the pictures here a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really, REALLY dig the picture for Project Metatron, and maybe it's silly of me, but that's what brings this up from a narrow downvote to a full upvote.

How did you make it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's so much blackboxing here it's very difficult to read coherently, let alone understand what's happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see where you're coming from, although the idea about the nukes was that they were getting desperate and were hoping to cause a cave-in/collapse on the other side.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just gonna second this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of removing something from continuity but having to leave enough information to avoid paradox is kind of interesting. Unfortunately too much of the story focused a not particularly well done inter-dimensional portal skip to really catch my interest. (although a portal that is primarily threatening due to pressure equalization could be kind of fun in its own right)

No-vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept, but it doesn't seem developed enough. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot of writing, and not enough interest to support it. I agree with Kalinin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Seemingly innocuous phenomenon that turns out to be world-destroying when you click the collapsibles" is a trick that I'm pretty burnt out on. I feel like I've seen it a ton in the last year or so. This isn't really a much different take on that concept, and in fact for the amount of text I'm reading through it's quite thin. There's a thing that's putting extra water into the oceans, the Foundation can't contain it, so it somehow erases it from history. I'm not an advocate of artificial article lengthening, but for something that destroys the world and necessitates fucking around with time itself, it's pretty bare bones in terms of story.

There's also a few dissonant notes in terms of narrative here for me. The idea of the Foundation firing nukes into this thing with seemingly no real objective other than to somehow blow up a dimensional anomaly. A dimensional anomaly, by the way, with a completely unknown location on the other side. The idea of an organization deploying nuclear weapons in such a haphazard manner shatters my personal threshold of rationality. I also found the "Project Metatron" title to be exceedingly goofy.

And no, I don't care for the use of blackboxing. It severely interrupts the flow of what's being read to the point of being exceedingly unpleasant. There has to be a better way of presenting those scraps of information. And on a minor note, the alloy used for the original containtment procedure being [REDACTED] obtained from [REDACTED AGAIN] struck me as kind of a poor use of censorship.

I feel like the concept of "secret Thaumiel thing that erases history," especially given the other comparable-style articles we already have that explore this subject, requires a lot more work than was put in here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see what you're going for, and I like it, but with the amount of blackboxing going on, I'm not actually sure whether or not I want to upvote or not. It's a matter of how you executed it (specifically all the blackboxing in the final document, when it seems like a bit too much).

EDIT: Upon further thinking, downvote. I gotta agree with quite a number of Kalinin's feedback, but I could let a lot of it slide if the last collapsible wasn't so incomprehensible and painful to read in a way that shuts the reader off from caring.

item.title: I accidentally the entire space-time continuum
item.content:encoded:
If a target were totally removed from spacee-████

I think that word has an e too many.

But it's a lovely story, and I personally think the erratic blackboxing enhances it. Here, have the +1 I promised when you posted the draft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely better. I'm hesitantly upvoting for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand that but wow I mean its like black boxed to hell and back. though this idea is very witty and such

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, like I said, that serves a narrative purpose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: its getting there still so so many black boxes for the other entity

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay I edited it somewhat. Should be more clear now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So basically this is the one that some how disappeared from all records if im following the idea behind this object?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly isn't there an article on the black boxes on how much is too much and what not?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thaumiel is an object that doesn't exist, the file was found in the SCP database. The black boxes in the last addendum are there for a very good reason related to the plot of the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am looking at them right now, open in another tab. Trust me, it's working. You probably need to CTRL+F5 before you see it.

After you upload images, the page refreshes, but you're only going to see the images on the page once you've refreshed again. What can I say? Wikidot is poop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand that you're using the blackboxes as an indication of data lost in the timebreak, but it's overused to the point of seriously harming the readability of the document. Especially when it's just erasing a letter or two in the middle of a word that is obvious in context. I would recommend fixing a lot of that and prefacing the docs with something like:

The following document is a heuristic analysis recovery of a document that was located with approximately 57% data corruption. Estimated accuracy of this document is 85%.

Let's you keep some of the blackboxes while not overwhelming the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Other then the image problem you have been having. Is this SCP suppose to be neutralized or Thaumiel. its not really clear to me but I think If I reread it would come to me . Also a little bit to much Blacked out text it covers up a lot of the lore and backstory to the SCP, I cant make heads or tails of what it is suppose to be exactly. its an island but is their something on it that makes it ultra dangerous or what is it. Basically what im saying is that their is way way too much blacked out text.
